verses.text: When the people had clean passed over Jordan, the LORD spake unto Joshua: Take twelve men out of the people, every tribe a man; command them, Take you hence out of the midst of Jordan, out of the place where the priests' feet stood firm, twelve stones, carry them over, leave them in the lodging place where ye shall lodge this night. Joshua called the twelve: Pass over before the ark into the midst of Jordan, take up every man a stone upon his shoulder, according to the number of the tribes — that this may be a sign among you. When your children ask in time to come, What mean ye by these stones? then ye shall answer, The waters of Jordan were cut off before the ark of the covenant of the LORD: these stones shall be for a memorial for ever. Guidance: God thinks in generations. Before the priests have even stepped out of the riverbed, He is planning a children's catechism. Twelve men, one per tribe, each shouldering a wet stone — every tribe carries a witness of the miracle home. A faith without memorials forgets within a generation.

The children of Israel did so as Joshua commanded, took up twelve stones out of the midst of Jordan, as the LORD spake unto Joshua, according to the number of the tribes, carried them over unto the place where they lodged, and laid them down there. Guidance: 'They did so as Joshua commanded.' One sentence — total compliance. Compare the wilderness generation's habit of negotiating with their leader. This generation lifts wet stones onto its shoulders without a complaint. Obedience has finally caught up to revelation.

Joshua set up twelve stones in the midst of Jordan, in the place where the feet of the priests which bare the ark of the covenant stood: they are there unto this day. Guidance: A second cairn — twelve stones submerged where the priests stood. Israel sees one set on land, but God knows another set lies hidden in the river. The visible memorial is for children's questions; the hidden one is for God's own remembrance. Some altars no human eye ever sees.

The priests which bare the ark stood in the midst of Jordan, until every thing was finished that the LORD commanded Joshua to speak unto the people: the people hasted and passed over. When all were clean passed over, the ark of the LORD passed over, and the priests, in the presence of the people. The children of Reuben, Gad, half Manasseh passed over armed before the children of Israel, as Moses spake: about forty thousand prepared for war passed over before the LORD unto battle, to the plains of Jericho. Guidance: The priests stand in mud while every command is finished. No shortcuts. Then the east-tribe warriors keep their word from Num 32 — 40,000 cross armed, leaving wives and children behind. The covenant is honored down to the count. Public promises require public payment.

On that day the LORD magnified Joshua in the sight of all Israel; they feared him, as they feared Moses, all the days of his life. Guidance: The promise of 3:7 fulfilled in one verse. Authority is conferred, not claimed. Joshua does not declare himself successor — God does, in front of the whole nation, by parting the river. And the awe lasts: 'all the days of his life.' A miracle at the start of a tenure buys decades of credit.

The LORD spake unto Joshua: Command the priests that bear the ark of the testimony, that they come up out of Jordan. Joshua commanded the priests, Come ye up out of Jordan. When the priests were come up out of the midst of Jordan, and the soles of the priests' feet were lifted up unto the dry land, the waters of Jordan returned unto their place, and flowed over all his banks, as they did before. Guidance: The miracle ends as it began — at the priests' feet. Step in, the water stops. Step out, the water returns. The Jordan resumes its flood the moment the last priest is on the bank. God's wonders are precisely timed: they start when faith enters, stop when faith exits, and never a second's overrun.

The people came up out of Jordan on the tenth day of the first month, and encamped in Gilgal, in the east border of Jericho. Those twelve stones, which they took out of Jordan, did Joshua pitch in Gilgal. Guidance: Tenth day, first month — the exact date the Passover lamb was chosen back in Exodus 12. Forty years to the calendar. Israel re-enters covenant space on the day they once selected the lamb that brought them out. Gilgal is the new Goshen — a base camp made of memorial stones.

He spake unto the children of Israel: When your children shall ask their fathers in time to come, What mean these stones? then ye shall let your children know, Israel came over this Jordan on dry land. For the LORD your God dried up the waters of Jordan from before you, until ye were passed over, as the LORD your God did to the Red sea, which he dried up from before us, until we were gone over: that all the people of the earth might know the hand of the LORD, that it is mighty: that ye might fear the LORD your God for ever. Guidance: The stones are catechism props. The catechism has two answers: the Jordan was opened, and the Red Sea was opened — same God, same hand, two generations. Joshua deliberately echoes Moses' language. Memorials are for two audiences: 'all the people of the earth' (witness), and 'ye for ever' (worship). One sign, two reaches.
